content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. girl movies that aren't feel good and rather very angry?

**Score:** 87
**Body:** im not talking like pitch perfect, mean girls etc. films like black swan, the virgin suicides, maybe the substance? basically tumblr girl films 😭

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hard Candy
2. Girl Interrupted
3. Thelma & Louise?
4. The Craft
5. Promising Young Woman might be peak this.
6. Maybe:

The Handmaiden  
Annihilation  
Loves Lies Bleeding  
Promising Young Woman  
The Lost Daughter  
Hustlers  
Widows  
Neon Demon  
Melancholia
7. I think Kill Bill volume I and II might fit this
8. Ready or Not

Send Help

Edit: I don't know why I didn't think of these in the moment...

Heathers

Bottoms
9. Suspiria

Black Narcissus 
10. Jennifer’s Body

## 2. “Fuck The Rich” Movies

**Score:** 70
**Body:** Due to…recent events, involving list and those who are on them I invited my girlfriend to a “fuck the rich” movie date night. I’m looking for movies that go into this theme. As I type this I’m watching “Ready or Not”, she suggested “The Menu”. Any other movies under this theme would be lovely, any genre is okay they don’t have to be action, thriller, horror, etc. just the core premise. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. A classic and comedic pinnacle of rich-fuckery: Trading Places
  - I rank it up there Brewster’s Millions as severely forgotten comedy masterpieces. Looks like I’m watching Jamie Lee’s tits this weekend for the hundredth time.
2. Triangle of Sadness
3. Parasite
4. Get out
5. Network (1976)
6. Sorry to Bother You
7. Trading places
8. Society
9. Being There (1979)

## 3. Feel-good movies for my 90 year old Grandma?

**Score:** 34
**Body:** Hey y'all! I've been spending much more time sitting and watching movies with my Grandma since my Grandpa passed back in November. It's been hard, but we've been getting through it as best as we can, and movies are a great little escape from reality when we need it.

Trouble is, I feel like we are genuinely running out of things to watch that she will actually enjoy! She dislikes more movies we turn on than she likes, unfortunately. The woman has her tastes! (as we all should by the time we're 90!)

Some movies/shows she's seen and loved (and will watch repeatedly if I let her!) : Fried Green Tomatoes, Steel Magnolias, O Brother Where Art Thou, Forrest Gump, pretty much any movie with Dolly Parton, The Sandlot, Shrek, The Little Rascals, Anne With An E, The Golden Girls, Secondhand Lions, most Disney movies from the 90's and older.

I've also gotten her to watch some good 90's and 2000's teen movies that I grew up with that she's enjoyed like Princess Diaries, Aquamarine, Ella Enchanted, Cadet Kelly, 13 Going on 30, Ice Princess, etc.

Okay....now....the things she has seen and called stupid because she doesn't like them: The Goonies, My Big Fat Greek Wedding, any of the Spiderman movies, anything even remotely considered horror (sigh), The Pagemaster, 50 First Dates, Young Sheldon, Ratatouille, and Paddington 1 and 2 are just a few I can name.

Please for the love of everything that is good and holy help me find movies to watch with my very picky old woman 😭


### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Now and Then

A League of their Own
  - A League of their Own is the perfect suggestion.
2. Maybe this is the wrong movie to show an old person, (because it's about old people?) but I thought the movie Thelma (2024) was lots of fun and light hearted.
3. Perhaps some movies from the 30s and 40s.   
Bringing Up Baby  
His Girl Friday  
It happened One Night  
Meet Me in St Louis
4. Big Fish
5. Babe
6. Nonnas
7. Chocolat
8. The Big Year with Steve Martin, Jack Black, and Owen Wilson. It's about a competition to see who can see the most birds. Really funny
9. Batteries not included

## 4. Funny "dumb" comedies like this is the end, anchorman, etc

**Score:** 19
**Body:** I've seen all the classics / superbad, pineapple express, step brothers, talladega nights, and so on. 

Is there any movies in that type of genre that you've seen that maybe aren't as much of a blockbuster hit but still a similar level of a funny comedy. Examples of lesser known movies like that would be movies like hotrod, rolemodels, drillbit taylor, the hot chick, dumb and dumber....

Just looking for suggestions! I feel like I've watched all the movies and really want a good one I haven't seen.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Grandma’s Boy
2. Hot Tub Time Machine is so much better than it has any right to be.
3. Walk Hard: The Dewey Cox Story (2007) is fairly well-known but I dont see it recommended as often as others!

Dodgeball (2004)

Tropic Thunder (2008) obv well known

Balls of Fury (2007)

Blades of Glory (2007)
  - It’s crazy how underrated Walk Hard is. It literally ruined all musician biopics for me (in a good way) . I can never watch a music biopic again because I’ll just laugh!!  It was too good!
4. Kung Pow! Enter The Fist
5. Better Off Dead
6. Kingpin
7. Airplane! 

Kentucky Fried Movie

Amazon Women On The Moon
8. Kung Fu Hustle
9. Wet Hot American Summer (and there's a Netflix series as well!). It's just sublimely stupid and funny, leaning into a lot of 80s summer camp comedy tropes.

## 5. Films about frendship

**Score:** 19
**Body:** Movies like
Perks of being a Wallflower
Dead Poets Society
My Girl 
Good Will Hunting
You get the idea, films about friends and something that leaves you thinking for weeks.
If anyone knows more films of this sub genre please help!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. stand by me is the ultimate friendship movie that'll mess you up for weeks, also the outsiders if you want to cry your eyes out
2. Me and Earl and the Dying Girl
3. The Cure (1995)

Now and Then (1995)

The Sandlot (1993)
4. Clerks 2

Swingers
5. School Ties, Love, Simon, Goonies, Now and Then, The Sisterhood of Traveling Pants, Juno, 13 Going on 30, The Curious Case of Benjamin Button.
6. the intouchables (2011)
7. Superbad
8. Steel Magnolias
9. Finding Forrester
10. Banshees of inesherin

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Why did The Lord of the Rings trilogy budget less than $300 million? Why was the cost so low for such a massive trilogy, while the original Spider-Man trilogy cost around $600 million and the Matrix trilogy around $400 million?

**Score:** 3778

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. In an unprecedented move, Peter Jackson shot all three movies at the same time.  While it created a long shooting schedule, it was arguably shorter than having three separate schedules.  They also had to build and maintain sets and other materials once.  There were literally times when Jackson shot a scene for early in The Fellowship of the Ring and then a few days later was shooting a scene for late in The Return of the King.  IMO, they also saved on actor salaries.  For example, both Cate Blanchett and Orlando Bloom have said they were paid relatively little to do the movies (both have stated doing the movies was life-changing and worth doing).
  - >There were literally times when Jackson shot a scene for early in The Fellowship of the Ring and then a few days later was shooting a scene for late in The Return of the King

Aside from the scenes in mount doom with frodo/smeagol/sam and ithilgar/elrond, are there other any other examples?
    - I saw something yesterday where McKellen said the last scene of the trilogy was the second scene he shot overall.
      - damn considering the emotion in the scene thats some damn good acting chops to pull off without the shared experience of the full shoot to build to
        - To be fair, we are talking about Sir Ian McKellen one of the best actors alive.

https://youtube.com/shorts/YtydyX6dRXU?si=Y7aG6ePD74EyVVl5
          - Sir Ian Sir Ian Sir Ian                                    Sir Ian Sir Ian
   

                               YOU SHALL NOT PASS
            - How did I know what to say? The words were written for me in a script. 

How did I know where to stand? Somebody told me.

😂😂😂
2. I read something about this, maybe I'm misremembering. if so, forgive me. After they locked in many of the costs in NZ dollars, the US dollar increased in value agains the NZ dollar. They were still on the hook for the same amount of NZ dollars, but those NZ dollars became much easier to pay off.   
  
As others have said, they didn't have to pay that much money to the actors (no Tom Cruise or Brad Pitts in the cast) and they made them all at once. Also they got massive support from the NZ government, literally the whole country. Not just financial support but they got massive value out of the fact that no one had really seen NZ in movies much so they could use it as an exotic location that wasn't a place people had seen 100 times already.
  - Middle earth did a lot more for New Zealand than Tatooine did for Tunisia. 

As much as they got good value out of the production New Zealand also got great returns from tourism and marketing that still benefit them to this day.
3. Everyone in the comments keeps on hitting at some particular points - but the full picture is that of lightning in a bottle. 

New Line Cinema was on the verge of insolvency - These films essentially represented potentially their final curtain. 

The films, as many have said, were shot as one. It was that the films were shot back to back, but were coordinated as one production. This simplified much of the logistics with scheduling. Simpler logistics means less money spent. 

Many of the actors were not massive money pits. New Line chose big name actors, but Lord of the Rings is an ensemble story - Told from many points of view. With an extensive cast and no one as the focus point the production was able to spread their finances out. 

Location, location, location! Lord of the Rings wasn't just shot in New Zealand - it was produced almost entirely in New Zealand. Many films baloon a budget from destination shoots. When they're done all of that equipment, crew, and materials has to be packed up and shipped back to their home studio, which at the time was normally in the United States. 

Not lord of the rings. 

The crew, artists, equipment, props and special effects were all done in New Zealand. The director was also the owner of Weta workshop who produced essentially everything for the movie from digital affects to prop weapons. Extras for larger battles scenes were all New Zealanders. There was also considerably less fiscal fluff with IATSE as it was not operating in America. 

The United States Dollar also goes a lot further in New Zealand. When I visited in 2014 it was damn near 1.00 USD to 0.50 NZD.

There were so many things that went right with those movies, it's little wonder why The Hobbit was unable to fill its sizeable shoes.

## 2. Why didn’t Orlando Bloom’s career take off like it seemed like it would?

**Score:** 1161
**Body:** I have had a ton of snow here since last weekend so I have had quite a bit of time to pass, and of course as someone who loves movies I passed it by rewatching a few of my favourites. Last week, it was The Lord of the Rings trilogy, and today on my lazy Sunday it was Kingdom of Heaven: Directors Cut. 

But this got me thinking, Orlando Bloom had an insane run in the early 2000’s and during that time seemed to be one of a few “go-to” choices for an actor who could pull off a role for a character in a historical/epic film. The Lord of the Rings, Troy, Pirates of the Caribbean, Kingdom of Heaven, and even Black Hawk Down.

 

So yes he may not have been the lead actor in any of these, except for Kingdom of Heaven, which by the way I think he did an excellent job in this movie as the main character. But he still did an excellent job and created a memorable performance for characters we will never forget such as Legolas, Will Turner, and Paris. It seemed like after landing roles in all of these huge scale and high budget movies that his career may take a trajectory not so dissimilar to Leo DiCaprio. I am NOT saying he is as good of an actor as Leo lol don’t worry, I merely wonder why after the early 2000’s he didn’t really appear in any other huge and popular movies, unless you count The Hobbit trilogy. 

I personally think he could have done a lot better in his later career so like from around 2010 to present day. He is a solid actor and has the looks and ability to pull off several different types of roles and characters that would be interesting and engaging to watch in big budget high level films. Just curious does anybody know if there was any controversy or trouble he may have gotten into to cause this decline in his career? Or I mean perhaps there’s a chance he just simply stopped pursuing big and significant roles maybe he was satisfied and content with what he accomplished so far in his career.. and that’s perfectly fine and reasonable. But look at guys like Leo, Brad Pitt, even Tom Cruise.. they had great roles when they were very young no doubt, but their careers also did not slow down or have drop in quality AT ALL during their late 30s, 40s, or even 50s tbh.  Just curious if anybody has any theories or insight about this. 

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Take off? What’s your definition. He did great.
  - He’s in three of the biggest franchises of all time, isn’t he?
    - And had a daughter with Katy Perry.
      - And Miranda Kerr...
        - Goddammit I wish I was rich and famous and attractive, and not repulsive
  - Orlando Bloom landed Legolas IMMEDIATELY after graduating from drama school! It’s literally every drama students dream! 

The audience may think that everyone wants to be Pitt, DeCaprio, or Clooney, but some folks find a good career and they do other things with their life. He still works, he just isn’t the thirstiest actor in Hollywood.
2. I’m sure he still gets money for Pirates & LOTR
3. He’s still fairly young, maybe he’ll get back into it. But maybe he was satisfied, he had a huge amount of good roles and publicity in the 2000s. Also, lots of actors and actresses do some roles and realize after bit that being in the spotlight is not for them.
  - Just watched a somewhat recent movie with him in it called “Deep Cover” nothing ground breaking but I thought it was really good and Orlando is super funny in it, honestly didn’t know he had comedic chops.
4. He fell out of a black Hawk helicopter in Mogadishu. Never bounced back from it.

## 3. The Banshees of Inisherin - Quiet, Cruel and Beautiful

**Score:** 1044
**Body:** With so much to unpack in this movie, my main fear is I won’t be able to articulate why I love it so much. The screenplay, without exaggeration, is one of my all-time favourites. It’s layered and deep story finds humour in the darkest corners of life. Colin Farrell’s performance will go down as an all-timer for me, I can say with full certainty. Not to take away anything from how equally wonderful Kerry Condon, Brendan Gleason and Barry Keoghan all are.

The cinematography is breathtaking. The score is beautiful. The performances, the costumes, the entire aesthetic of the film are all just wonderful. It almost sneaks up on you how incredible the whole package is.

It’s that rare comfort film that is dark, cruel and offers very little hope. But there’s something so beautiful in  how unforgiving it is. It is the rarest of masterpieces, in my very humble opinion.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Cruel af.
2. The donkey man… the donkey. I teared up after that scene.
  - Poor Jenny
    - Priest: Do you think God gives a damn about miniature donkeys, Colm?

Colm Doherty: I fear he doesn't. And I fear that's where it's all gone wrong.
3. I think I mostly remember the cruelty.
  - It’s cruel, but it offers real insight into human connection, relationships and loneliness.
    - It's about the Irish civil war and how it drove people who once were friends and family to turn on each other.
4. "Thanks for looking after me dog."

"Anytime."

Absolutely broke me, not going to lie.
5. That poor boy just wanted to find love…
  - “Well there goes that dream…”

## 4. Jack Nicholson’s raw intensity elevated this monologue into something truly memorable. [A Few Good Men]

**Score:** 927
**Body:** Scene from A Few Good Men (1992).

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You sleep under a blanket of freedom and then question the manner in which I provide it? 

I don't give a damn what you think you are entitled to!

He really dropped the hammer in this movie.
  - He has less than 15 minutes of screen time and spent less than 10 days on set.

But he became the most iconic part of that movie.
2. The performance is so good it’s overshadowed the words themselves, which is a remarkable depiction of how people addicted to military power see themselves vs the world
  - Jessup doesn't believe the military is right, he believes he is right. That's why he constantly shows disrespect to other military members including the judge.

He isn't dangerous simply because he is a tough military man defending the nation, he is dangerous because he believes he is more important than the military, than civilians, than morals or doing the right thing. In his own mind he is the only one that matters.
3. And apparently he stayed on set to perform for the actors even when he wasn’t in the shot, which isn’t always common 
4. Despite being a grade A bastard, Jessup is eloquent as fuck.
  - Yeah, Jessup was evil, and Nicholson really played the role brilliantly.
  - I think many people understand that this is wrong and bad, but the *reasons* why aren't commonly taught or understood and so this type of personality — like that of The Wolf of Wall St — end up socially cheered and defended. Jessup got to make his argument, and while it was taken as a loss for Jessup, the *argument* remained strongly put and unchallenged.
5. Sorkin is just the absolute king of dialogue and heavy-hitting monologue, Social Network, Newsroom, West Wing, Moneyball.  

Each one is remarkably well written
  - He should be a writer.

## 5. Name one movie moment where you’d be dead in seconds if it happened in real life.

**Score:** 721

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Marty McFly climbing into his room in the alternate timeline.
  - FREEZE SUCKA!!!
2. Hiding in a refrigerator to escape a nuclear blast

Unprotected in space
3. In Home Alone 2 when Harry & Marv get in the face with a large heavy metal pipe.
  - They should have died MULTIPLE times in the first one...
    - Kevin committed a lot of murder in those movies

![gif](giphy|ZIv3CQzI1R2hPWiPIL)
4. Fast and the furious.  All the movies, all the scenes.  Like literally everything.
  - You never had me
5. Commando is literally the greatest film in the history of the moving image.

But one dude single handedly taking on an entire military force. He kills 100 army guys, easily 🤣
6. John Wick falling from atop the Continental hotel in John Wick 3. Awnings won't do much if you fall from about 13-14 stories. I mean imagine the internal bleeding, ruptured organs and overall trauma...

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. How Kubrick directed; as described by a dispassionate but close and detailed witness

**Score:** 137
**Body:** [https://scrapsfromtheloft.com/movies/writing-the-shining-by-diane-johnson/](https://scrapsfromtheloft.com/movies/writing-the-shining-by-diane-johnson/)

Kubrick's workflow, thoughts and 'process' -- outside being on set, surrounded by cast and crew -- are shrouded in a kind of mystique. Kubrick co-wrote or adapted almost everything he ever made, but for some reason we as a culture are still enraptured by figuring out the inside of his mind. Superficial questions about him abound. What was he trying to depict precisely in *2001*? (This despite Arthur C Clarke publishing a novel based on the same treatment which basically unlocks all of the film's exposition). Why did he choose relatively obscure literary works like *Clockwork Orange* and *Barry Lyndon* by otherwise well-known novelists to stake millions of dollars in production in? What even was Kubrick's taste as a consumer of fiction? Why was he so obsessed with adapting the novel that would become *Eyes Wide Shut* (which he seemed to be planning at least from the 70s)?

I find it fascinating, therefore, that in 2018 some kind of small film blog site called *Scraps From The Loft* published an interview with Diane Johnson, who co-wrote *The Shining's* script with him, and she lays bare the most technical, efficient and specific details of Kubrick's style as a writer that I've seen. It's basically the legend to understanding Kubrick as a writer and editor that isn't clearly established elsewhere.

Why did Kubrick adapt King's book when he doesn't seem to have liked very much about it? Johnson (whose own largely forgotten 1970's novel was the other option Kubrick was considering for adapting as his first horror movie and is how he found her a co-writer) posits a purely logistical reasoning -- a third person narrative with enough psychological content to allow Kubrick a canvas to project his own Freudian and Gothic styles onto. He told Johnson, as consolation for why he wasn't adapting her novel, that he preferred to adapt 'less literary' works into films. With *Clockwork* and *Barry Lyndon*, she says he deliberately chose lesser-known works by well-known authors.

Johnson says Kubrick was mindlessly consuming genre fiction at huge volumes in the 70s to find the work to adapt. He otherwise seemed to stick with literary fiction -- Johnson remarks that she was taken aback by Kubrick's level of literary knowledge, herself a professor.

King famously hated the way Wendy Torrance was reduced to a nothing character by the film, and Johnson says her script contained a more fulsome character for Wendy; Kubrick tells Johnson he filmed such a supposedly bland female main character because Shelley Duvall could not say the lines written.

Johnson says she and Kubrick spent evenings watching older Jack Nicholson performances trying to decide which of his 'modes' of acting were better -- is this why Kubrick seems to have been unable to modulate Nicholson's performance in earlier scenes to depict a more clear decline into madness? Because he'd watched so much Nicholson that he could no longer detect when he was been over the top (to some)? Johnson doesn't presume.

Finally, Johnson lays bare the conceptualisation of Kubrick as a formalist overly intellectual and highly controlling director; she clarifies that his process (as she saw it) of only having a single film on the go at once allowed him to essentially direct the movie from home before the first day of shooting. She watches as he goes deep on every element of the film before the script is finished, down to the tiling on the bathroom sets, with input sought from members of his family and Johnson. It makes perfect sense, then, that Kubrick on set is task-driven, not actor-focussed, and not open to compromise: he's already planned every aspect of the film a year earlier. He for example wants 150 takes because he spent months watching Jack Nicholson in every movie he ever did, and he's aware what kind of line delivery he needs and Nicholson is capable of but cannot articulate it.

Her memories of co-writing the script don't even seem to depict Kubrick as some grand high-faluting auteur of filmic theory or of metaphor, theme, and visual language. It's more that Kubrick so obsessively breaks down every element of every frame and that this total control (at the highest and lowest levels) is from where Kubrick 'directed'. The famous patterned carpet in the Overlook might have once had a deeper meaning in Kubrick's mind, one that tied the movie together, but by the time he's physically on set he's long incorporated that into his blueprint for the movie that it's not something he pontificates about.

There's even more in the essay that I haven't mentioned -- how Kubrick's need for realism clashed with a supernatural story (he worked it out in the end), how he seems to already be directing from the first time he picks up the phone and calls his future co-writer.

I'm not pretending this is the first time a collaborator of Kubrick reminisced about working with him, but for some reason to me it seems like Johnson is the most effective at capturing the how and why without too much effort to get inside Kubrick's head; I have no semblance of her writing career outside *The Shining* but in 2018 she basically wrote the decoder ring for Kubrick and few people noticed.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Very revealing essay. What it clarifies for me is that Kubrick’s rigor was never about encoding meaning but about destabilizing it. A telling quote from Johnson

“He was quite capable of living with the paradox of something being both true and untrue at the same time.”

This kind of paradox a lot of modern “Kubrick descendants” aren’t super comfortable with. For example, Nolan and Villeneuve both borrow the cold, calculated compositions, the austere framing (also the sense of seriousness), but they use it to produce legible meaning and stability to their images. Any kind of ambiguity (the spinning top at the end of Inception or Arrival’s time loop) still feels intentional and ultimately bounded in a very legible system. 

This is the divergence that makes Kubrick a great artist. So much prestige cinema imitates his style without the more interesting “dangerous” aspects of it.
  - >something being both true and untrue at the same time.

I thought that was interesting because Kubrick got his start producing highly composed artful 'candid' photography for magazines like *Life* \-- things like capturing the moment a young couple are caught making out on a fire escape, sleeping on the train, or a woman slapping her grabby boyfriend in the cinema. 

But of course, these photos were largely manufactured. Even when he came across an interesting candid subject or subjects, Kubrick would pose and compose them in the best way, or find the most dramatic action or moment. He was always producing fiction and manufacturing drama and his compositions always had that sense of sterility and affectation that would see him reproducing entire Manhattan blocks painstakingly on a soundstage (down to the garbage and traffic lights) for *Eyes Wide Shut* a half century later.
    - I’m not 100% sure if this is the contradiction you’re pointing out, but perhaps through his early Life photography, he (maybe even instinctively) recognised that giving it form and therefore meaning through that form moved it further and further away from the “candid” value those photos were supposed to represent. He reacted to that by making films so saturated in “form” that it serves to destabilise meaning instead of enable it. Eyes Wide Shut, especially, I think is a film about a world where meaning is already exhausted and hollowed out - and asks the question “How do you continue functioning here”. In a way, there is no meaningful difference (for Kubrick’s cinema) between the real New York street and the (however meticulously) artificially redesigned version of it.
  - I think Jonathan Glazer is a better example of a post Kubrick director for his combination of detached, voyeuristic formalism with some unspoken narrative ambiguity and an acknowledgment of an outside influence or larger system, as can be seen in both Under the Skin and Zone of Interest. 

The story is not necessarily directly "told" so much as it is laid out out before the viewer, without handholding or accompanying instructions.
    - (I haven’t seen Under the Skin in a long time so I can’t comment on it)

To me, Zone of Interest feels radically different from Kubrick. With Kubrick, I’m seeing an inverse relationship between form and meaning, but with Glazer, his form already perfectly contains his ethical posture. The film refuses drama, sure, but meaning is pre-sorted - which is really my issue with modern Kubrick “descendants”. The “danger” with Zone of Interest is that it’s an exercise in anti-cinema, but it still has an ideological comfort that Kubrick (in his better films) refuses.
  - Thank you 🙏 somebody had to say it. I actually love Nolan, and really like Villeneuve, I personally have no problem with them. But the issue with contemporary directors in general is the way they are deified by cinephiles. The newer generation of filmmakers are undeniably excellent craftspeople but very rarely are they offering up something to say that challenges us and affects the way we think on a deep, lasting level. I would argue that Nolan has some interesting things to say, but he definitely lacks the subtlety and nuance that Kubrick offers.
    - This! Thank you, this is exactly how I’ve felt lately about the newer generation of directors
2. >Why did he choose relatively obscure literary works like *Clockwork Orange* and *Barry Lyndon* by otherwise well-known novelists to stake millions of dollars in production in?

Was *Clockwork* obscure? It was at least fairly well-known as a controversial, boundary-pushing novel. And it's certainly Anthony Burgess's best-known work by several orders of magnitude.
3. The 2001 theories and discussions always get me because it seems like nobody in the Kubrick subs actually read the book. You’ll get all sorts of wild stuff listed about that movie when it’s right there in the book. Not only that, but if someone liked the world that 2001 built, Clarke when in to write several sequels.
  - The book and the film were developed at the same time, and the film ended up being more Kubrick's vision while the book is Clarke's. So what's true in one isn't necessarily true in the other.

## 2. I just watched The Lives of Others and…

**Score:** 52
**Body:** When HGW opened the book at the end and saw the dedication absolutely broke me.  I was not ready and haven’t cried from a film like that in years.

I went into this film thinking it was going to be a sort of One Hour Photo vibe, knowing little about it.  It turned out to be about stifled lives straining to express themselves under a smothering atmosphere, with a desperately lonely man who ultimately works in the shadows for them.

Seeing HGW delivering mail at the end before the bookstore, just a normal man who made a sacrifice, made me think of the quote, “Every day, in every walk of life, ordinary people do extraordinary things.”

I did not know how it would all wrap up, but bravo.  Beautiful, beautiful film.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Want to have your mind blown? Before the wall fell 25% of East German citizens were informants for the Stasi. The guy playing Wiesler, Ulrich Mühe, was a celebrated East German theatre actor. After reunification he went to review his own Stasi file, and discovered his own wife was informing on him
  - According to Wikipedia “… Gröllmann's real-life controller later claimed he had made up many of the details in the file and that the actress had been unaware that she was speaking to a Stasi agent.”
    - Well that’s an interesting twist
  - That's incredible..!
2. It's one of my favourite film endings of all time. I think this last phrase in the fil?, "No, this is for myself", carries so many layers. It reveals the motivation for this extraordinary act of concealment at the film's centre. It shows the man as an individual, liberated from being a cog in a machine. And of course the book is dedicated to him. It's about him, in many ways.
3. Amazing film. Anyone interested in Cold War history or the legacy of the Stasi who finds themselves in Berlin should consider touring Hohenschönhausen Prison, which is the secret Stasi facility shown in the film. When I visited, they still had a few tour guides who had been held there previously as political prisoners.

## 3. 'Friendship' (2024), funny or depressing?

**Score:** 27
**Body:** So this is definitely one of the films I was most curious to check out last year, I wouldn't say I'm the biggest Tim Robinson/ITYSL fan but I have seen the whole series and I can say that when it lands, it absolutely cracks me up, but I also find it to be somewhat hit or miss.

That being said, I was also curious to see how his style would translate to a feature rather than the sketch world. After seeing it, my friend pointed out it was as though the film was what would happen if you took a character from one of the sketches but they didn't get the grace of having the scenario end after 3-4 minutes; they had to live with the consequences of how shitty and unreasonable they were.

To that end, the film played its hand far straighter than I anticipated, which lead to the experience being less funny than I expected, though not in a bad way; I got something far different out of the film than I originally expected, which was something a lot more dry and sad than anything else.

I should say, I think part of it may have to do with the kind of crowd you see it in; I'm sure most of the festival audiences are the kind that would be more familiar with Tim's work and subsequently more receptive to it, but I saw this film with some friends at an arthouse theater where the majority of the audience seemed to be significantly older (I guess they were there for Paul Rudd?) but they barely laughed at anything in the film - with the exception of Connor O'Malley's Afghanistan joke, which genuinely did get a full belly laugh from the whole room.

In general, I feel like I tend to suffer a bit more from the 'secondhand embarrassment' phenomenon which makes the 'cringe comedy' stuff harder to watch. Most of the ITYSL sketches are just fully absurd but this film was played just realistic enough to just make it legitimately uncomfortable. 

That's not to say it was entirely without laughs, there would be random choices such as the song they listen to as they initially drive up to the sewers which made me crack up (just the image of these two 40 somethings driving around at night listening to 2000s butt rock was perfect) and of course the Subway sequence had me dying, but in general, the film was just a far more cold and unpleasant experience than I would have expected based on early responses and reviews. 

The tone almost reminded me somewhat of *The Curse*, the television show with Nathan Fielder, which also feels like taking concepts from a 'cringe comedy' setup and executing them so dryly that it essentially turns into psychological drama. 

I'm curious if anyone else felt the same way about this. Would consider rewatching eventually but I'd definitely have to be in the right frame of mind lol.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I cannot believe that scene where Tim goes nuts during the meeting about Mayor Nichols' campaign didn't bring the house down. That is the funniest scene i have seen in a movie in many years.
2. I'd say Friendship definitely falls under the label of dark comedy. It's a good example of how comedy doesn't always mean laugh-out-loud pratfalls, sometimes it means characters being placed in exaggerated/strange situations. But it still had a lot of moments that I found funny in a goofball way, like the drug trip scene or the police officers trying to push the dog into the sewer.  
  - The moment he realizes he lost his wife till the sequence of him getting the police in the sewers to find her is the funniest part of the entire film.
3. Have you checked out The Chair Company? It’s the same tone and I think it’s better suited to episode format, there’s time to build suspense, callback to earlier jokes and you just to enjoy the cast oscillating between logic and insanity. 

I did enjoy Friendship though: 
“Where’s mom?”

“. . . . She’s in the sewer.”
  - The Chair Company was a great ride but I didn’t think they landed the plane at all.
    - With that I don't really think they intend to ever necessarily, which is what frustrates me with it. However, I did have it pointed out to me that that's probably the point? Which is a fair posit/intention, until it's inevitably cancelled and they make up some nonsense.
4. I liked it enough. I found it REALLY depressing and at times really hilarious. 

I almost always love Tim’s stuff - so I’m definitely the audience for it. 

But I also think it leaned too heavily into the worst of his humor. Him just yelling as the joke is funny a few times. But it felt like a really funny sketch idea with an identity crisis when stretched out to a film length. I also think he’s just too annoying of a character to want to spend that much time with - and the weird disconnect between his bizarre ITYSL behavior with a normal drama landed kinda flat. 

I think the chair company was a much better execution of his humor paired with drama. He’s odd and strange but he had glimmers of a functioning human so his existence in the world doesn’t seem entirely at odds with it.
5. I liked it. Didnt think it was AMAZING but did think it was good.  Of note to me that it had degrees of absurdity to the characters. Normally just have the totally normal person/group and the weird one, but in this movie Paul Rudd’s character also fairly exaggerated/whacky, just not as much as Tim.
6. I think I saw it labeled as "cringe comedy" which made me understand it better and appreciate it a little more but was still mad I watched it the whole way through as opposed to giving up after the first act 🤷🏻‍♂️
7. I am smack dab in the middle of Tim Robinson's target audience. I love his style and everything from Detroiters, to ITYSL, to Chair Company I really love. The showing I went to had a very receptive audience too, so lots of laughs throughout the whole thing.

## 4. Eddington: exhaustion, dark comedy, and political neutrality.

**Score:** 25
**Body:** Finally caught up with Eddington and found myself much higher on it than the general consensus, largely because I think it’s being misread as ponderous or evasive when it’s actually doing something quite specific.

Yes, the runtime feels long, arguably oppressively so, but that duration feels intentional. The film mirrors the temporal drag and psychic fatigue of the pandemic period rather than offering narrative efficiency or release. It’s less a political thesis than a portrait of psychological attrition.

I was struck by the emotional sincerity is. Ari Aster shoots the film with a grim, almost clinical empathy, and the three leads are uniformly excellent: Joaquin Phoenix plays collapse as slow erosion rather than explosion; Pedro Pascal weaponizes charisma while remaining curiously hollow; and Emma Stone delivers a restrained, quietly devastating performance that anchors the film’s moral weight.

The gunfire is brutally designed, flat, concussive, and shocking. Each shot feels invasive and final, with the silence that follows doing as much damage as the sound itself. I want all movie gunfire to sound like this.

I was also surprised by how darkly funny the film is. Not satire in the conventional sense, but humor born out of behavioral absurdity, people clinging to slogans, certainty, and performative belief as meaning collapses around them. The laughs curdle quickly, which feels appropriate rather than indulgent.

Very curious to know if this movie resonated with others, and if not why not. Was there humour there for anyone else. Especially interested those who bounced off it hard, why?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Movie of the year for me. The chaos was well represented in the political divide and conquer tactics big money, goldenmagikarp, deploy to quietly screw everyone.

Setting it in the COVID era made the absurdity believable since we've witnessed it.
  - I’m so disappointed I didn’t go see jt. So certain it would have landed even heavier on me. But yeah, top tier movie in my opinion. Thoroughly entertaining, funny, with something to say about the world. Stunned by how much hate it got.
2. Very solid synopsis IMO. 

As you said, it’s not a political thesis but a portrait of reaction and social decay. Each protagonist is a vehicle of it. Phoenix the reactionary right winger, Pascal the corporate liberal phony, and Stone the anti intellectual pseudo free-thinker.
  - Ari Aster drops an original film with three killer performances from actors everyone loves, takes a big swing at dissecting the modern world… and somehow everyone hates it? That’s wild to me.
    - It’s definitely not the case that everyone hates it. A loooot of people* are calling it one of the best movies of the last year. 

*(I mean, not the Oscars, but they didn’t even nominate OPN’s Marty Supreme soundtrack, so what do they know)

Within a month or so of when it came out, I saw one critic call it the most effective satire of this more atomized, 2020s version of society that they’ve ever seen. 

I do think that Aster makes movies that are upsetting and alienating, and I think that’s on purpose. Obviously not everyone will love movies like that. I don’t mean that condescendingly either, because as much as I was in awe of it, I wasn’t exactly jumping to see it again either. Several scenes near the end disturbed me in ways most horror movies never manage to.
    - Because it’s kinda true in a way, albeit fiction; and truth doesn’t exist in people’s mind anymore. Disinformation has taken over. 
3. I sort of a have a theory on this movie that your enjoyment / appreciation of it is directly linked to your ability to look COVID in the eye. I think some people really still have a hard time coming to terms with how transformative COVID was, and those might be the first people to write the movie off as nihilistic or whatever. I think it really will age well, and it’s amazing how it avoided being about “isolation”… because, well, duhh. And I think what really stressed people out about COVID was the times they DID come together with people and realized how much people had changed. Truly scary and Aster gets that. 

For instance i think a lot of people were reticent to admit how scary the simple act of wearing a mask, and walking around and everyone in a mask, was. This movie shows that brilliantly. It was weird as fuck to only see people’s eyes! And funny. 

I absolutely adored Deirdre Oconnells performance as ES’s mom. Insane. Reminds me of Aster’s short film Basically. 

The only element of the film I was wary of was I think a little bit of the cynicism towards young white people. I know it sounds weird to say that, but as a black person I found 2020 to be really interesting and did experience some white people who were really confronted with their identity for the first time. So I think going cynical with that is a little short sighted, tho I do get it.
4. I feel similarly. Need to rewatch to formulate my thoughts. I left the theater disappointed but have found myself thinking about this film so much in the months since, and that’s worth something.
  - I wish I had have seen this in the theater. My sound system is nice, but nothing beats cinema quality.
    - Just replying here to tell you that:

>A portrait of psychological attrition

Is the most apt descriptor I've yet seen for this film.

## 5. How to start appreciating and "understanding" more critically acclaimed, "classical" films?

**Score:** 20
**Body:** I really like movies, but in some instances, when watching classical or very highly regarded movies, i tend not to see anything "special" in it, and i cant help but think that im missing out. One of my favorite movies is "In Bruges", and i feel that, for me to like a work of art in film, a lot must happen in the plot and visually, and i must get the "point" of the movie. I dont know if im making myself clear enough. Sometimes, like when i watched "La Haine", i was a bit bored about the happenings of the movie, feeling like i wasnt stimulated enough. How can i start to introduce myself to more "complex" and "slow" movies, like "Stalker" by Tarkovski? (sorry if the movie isnt really like that, thats only my impression of it wihout seeing it). Thank you for the attention!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. A lot of movies, especially classic films, you have to have a bit of perspective for when it came out. I’m not sure what the word would be for film, but for social studies we call it “historical empathy”.

So you need to start to Think about why is that movie acclaimed. Was the cinematography innovative? Was it the magnum opus of a famous actor or director? Did it touch on something controversial or challenge the norms of the time? Is it a time capsule that reflected the era? Was the costumes, set designs and music unique?  Was it a runaway sleeper hit or a movie that was originally disliked but gained acclaim either? Was it genre defining or did it begin a new trend? 

Appreciating a movie is much like wine tasting. You aren’t just looking at the flavor of the wine. You are also focusing on the color, the smell, the subtle notes, the mouthfeel, the price versus quality, the uniqueness versus its competition. the history, location and reputation of the winery may even give some bias.

For a movie it’s the same way. You look at all the different parts that go into making the movie. You use all your senses and emotions and step back and see how all those aspects blend together. 

At the end of the day what you enjoy is what you enjoy. You can’t force yourself to like a film. And don’t let people “peer pressure” you into liking something. Not everything has to be deeply analyzed art. Sometimes you just want to watch a movie for the sake of watching a movie. I think a lot of people over analyze to try to prove som sort of intellectualism. Which it IS good to think about deeper themes and what not, but life doesn’t need to be so serious 24/7.

Even if it wasn’t your thing, but you still got some enjoyment out of it, even if it was just the cinematography, performances, or even atmosphere) or it got you thinking a bit, then keep it in mind and watch it again later with fresh eyes and mind. Maybe when you are in the mood for that type of movie.

I can’t tell you how many times I didn’t like or “get” a movie upon first viewing, but my opinion of it changed later after I had more life experiences under my belt and seen many more movies of a similar genre or vibe.

Our taste and interest  is subtly changing all the time. So don’t beat yourself up if you think your “doing it wrong”
2. >a lot must happen in the plot

I think this is the thinking that needs to be expanded/re-framed.

Sometimes a narrative can have a lot happening in it, even if there's not a lot happening in "the plot". Sometimes, instead, there's a lot happening on a thematic level, or in the character relationships, or in how the narrative is responding to the broader societal context of its time, etc.

The narratives that stand the test of time are often ones that are rich with themes, so sometimes they are less plot-driven as a result. A lot of classic films will fall into this bucket.

Think less about *what* is happening in the film and more about all the various ways of asking why and how. Why is it happening? Why did the filmmaker put it there? How are they showing this to us? How are they expecting us to feel?
  - I think this is where most people, myself included, struggle to make the jump from movies as purely entertainment to film as art, and so don't fully get why some movies are as critically acclaimed as they are. Most people need "plot" and struggle to engage with subtler aspects if the plot doesn't go very far.
3. This night seem counterintuitive because I'm going to suggest a different medium.

If you read literature in novel form, you will learn patience and beyond that, learn about different ways of seeing and experiencing the world, and of different ways of telling stories, and that some experiences and stories are too complex to have easy, simple answers. Like life. 

The same goes with film. The more different viewpoints you are exposed to, the more you learn about the world, and history, and different cultures, and you can learn to appreciate that with which you are unfamiliar, and have the patience and grace to be able to accept ambiguity and the existence of simultaneous contradictory truths, depending on a person's viewpoint.
4. I'm guessing you're relatively young? I think this is largely an attention span thing. Both social media and modern traditional media have increased the pace with which we consume stuff and I think that's damaged people's ability to sit still, so to speak. I really think if you want to appreciate the classics you'll need to train yourself to be able to deal with slow films, feel the space between the lines, take the time to look at the screen, the shot composition, the characters, everything. It's funny, in my mind La Haine isn't even particularly old.
5. When you watch these classic movies, do you just watch them straight up, or do you read anything before or after watching them?

A lot of these movies are considered classics because of their context in film, history, or what they’re saying, which could also be abstract if you’re not already clued in.

Like, La Haine is a movie I really enjoyed, but understanding that this film was inspired by the police shooting a man already subdued and handcuffed to a radiator, and that there were riots in France, in part because of police brutality, color in how our characters act that might not be explicit, even though they mention riots happening at the beginning of the movie.

Another part of it is that a lot of movies considered classics can be slower, and require you to consider more than just the plot. Stalker for example, has a very minimal plot despite its runtime. If you’re not actively thinking about what the characters are saying and what they’re philosophizing leading to their intentions, then it’s a very boring movie.
6. Don't force yourself to enjoy movies you don't, just explore, find new stuff you like and be open to anything, pay attention to what you're watching and rewatch. This will develop your curiosity and feel for the movies you're watching, and you'll gain a richer understanding of what movies you find impactful. Your taste will expand. For movies you find boring, still try to identify what you like and don't like. Be attentive to your experience watching movies.
7. If you like In Bruges maybe check out older black comedies like Dr Strangelove, Network, A Fish Called Wanda, Raising Arizona, The King of Comedy, Brazil. They're all very accessible and entertaining.

Also maybe try out  films that, like Bruges, are noted for their great comic dialogue - Rosencratz and Guildenstern are Dead, Withnail and I, Sideways, the 1970s Woody Allen comedies?

More generally, I think a great way for you expand your taste would be to write a list of films you love and find out which films and directors influenced them, and then go back and watch those films. That will then in turn open the door to more stuff.
8. Not every movie is accessible to everyone or every age/experience level. There are classic movies that I watched as a teen/young adult where I didn't "get" why they were classics, but after I'd been in the world longer and had new experiences and was exposed to a variety of new things/places/people/media/etc I came back to these films and immediately felt those things that went over my head or slipped right by me. The ability to detect nuance is sometimes something that only comes with time and living.
9. Something that worked pretty well for me was starting with very old films, stuff from 100+ years ago, then progress forward from there. You get to see all the technology and technique they **didn't** have available. Then, as you move forward in time you get to see it develop. You get to see the groundbreaking ideas as they develop, and how they made films different from before.

If you're not super young, you may have already appreciated the use of drone cameras to get shots that would have been incredibly difficult if not impossible before, and available to much smaller budgets.
